title: 'Salem's Lot
author: Stephen King
description: King's take on the small-town vampire story. The town itself is the best character — he takes his time introducing everyone, and it pays off once things start going wrong.

title: 77 Shadow Street (with bonus novella The Moonlit Mind)
author: Dean Koontz
description: A haunted-building story where the building is stranger than any ghost. Not Koontz's warmest cast, but the mystery of the Pendleton kept me turning pages.

title: Always Postpone Meetings with Time-Wasting Morons (Dilbert #1)
author: Scott Adams
description: Early Dilbert. I keep a few of these around for whenever I need a quick laugh at the office's expense.

title: Bag of Bones
author: Stephen King
description: King on grief and writer's block — by reputation one of his gentler, sadder books. Saving it for the right mood.

title: Brain Games Mini - Games and Puzzles on the Go: Make Trips More Fun with Puzzles and Games
author: Publications International LTD,Brain Games
description: A little book of puzzles for travelling. Not literature, just good fun for a long drive.

title: Cows of Our Planet (Far Side Collection)
author: Gary Larson
description: More Far Side. The cow panels are some of Larson's best work, and that is a high bar.

title: Dark Rivers of the Heart
author: Dean Koontz
description: A Koontz thriller I haven't started yet. His chase stories rarely disappoint me.

title: Dragon Tears
author: Dean Koontz
description: A Koontz thriller that moves at full speed from the first chapter. Not his deepest, but the good people in it are genuinely good, which is why I keep coming back to him.

title: Dragons of Eden
author: Carl Sagan
description: Sagan on how human intelligence came to be. Some of the science has aged, but the wonder hasn't — this was a re-read and it held up.

title: Forward the Foundation
author: Isaac Asimov
description: Asimov's farewell to Seldon. It was moving the first time I read it, so I'm saving the re-read for last.

title: Foundation
author: Isaac Asimov
description: Where it all began — one of my favourite series since childhood. I'm re-reading the whole saga in order, starting here.

title: Foundation and Earth
author: Isaac Asimov
description: The furthest point of the saga. Re-reading in order so I arrive here properly, the way I did the first time.

title: Foundation and Empire
author: Isaac Asimov
description: The Mule made a big impression on me the first time round. Looking forward to meeting him again.

title: Foundation's Edge
author: Isaac Asimov
description: Asimov's return to the series after thirty years. I read it long ago; curious whether the shift in style feels different now.

title: Friday
author: Robert A. Heinlein
description: Heinlein's late-career spy story. He's a favourite from way back, so this one has been on the list a while.

title: Fugitive from the Cubicle Police (Dilbert #8)
author: Scott Adams
description: More Dilbert. Office comedy that never really goes out of date.

title: Hound of The Far Side
author: Gary Larson
description: Larson at his weird best. A few panels got a proper laugh out of me, which is rare for a cartoon book.

title: I, Robot
author: Isaac Asimov
description: The stories that gave us the Three Laws. I read them as a child and they shaped what I expect robots — and robot stories — to be.

title: Just After Sunset
author: Stephen King
description: A King short story collection. Uneven the way collections always are, but the best ones stayed with me for days.

title: Night of the Crash-Test Dummies
author: Gary Larson
description: Classic Larson absurdity. I read it slowly to make it last.

title: Prelude to Foundation
author: Isaac Asimov
description: A young Hari Seldon. The prequels came later, but I'll take any excuse to spend more time in this universe.

title: Rogue Lawyer
author: John Grisham
description: Grisham in scrappy street-lawyer mode. His books are reliable company, which is exactly what I want sometimes.

title: Saint Odd
author: Dean Koontz
description: I've always enjoyed the Odd Thomas series. This one is no exception. Starts with the same high as the others.

title: Brother Odd
author: Dean Koontz
description: The third Odd Thomas book, set in a snowed-in monastery. Odd is one of the most decent characters in fiction, and the quieter setting suits him.

title: Second Foundation
author: Isaac Asimov
description: The close of the original trilogy. I remember the ending fondly and want to see if it still lands the same way.

title: Sole Survivor
author: Dean Koontz
description: A Koontz mystery built on an unexplainable plane crash. The premise alone earned it a spot on the list.

title: Surely You're Joking, Mr. Feynman!
author: Richard P Feynman
description: Feynman telling stories on himself. Everyone I trust says it's as much fun as science books get.

title: The Bad Place
author: Dean Koontz
description: Koontz with a stranger-than-usual mystery — a man afraid of his own sleep. The Dakotas are easy to root for, and Thomas might be the most wholesome character Koontz ever wrote.

title: The Best of the Best: 20 Years of the Year's Best Science Fiction
author: Robert Silverberg
description: Twenty years of the best short science fiction in one volume. One to dip into between novels rather than read straight through.

title: THE BICENTENNIAL MAN
author: Isaac Asimov
description: Asimov's robot who wanted to be human — one of the gentlest ideas in science fiction. I loved it growing up and expect it to hold up.

title: The Chickens are Restless
author: Gary Larson
description: You'd think the Far Side formula would wear thin by now. It doesn't.

title: The City (with bonus short story The Neighbor)
author: Dean Koontz
description: So far this is an easy read with likeable, easy to empathise with characters. A little mystery and a whole lot of life. I am thoroughly enjoying this.

title: The Curse Of Madame C
author: Gary Larson
description: Late-era Far Side, still sharp. Perfect bedtime reading, two pages at a time.

title: The Dilbert Principle: A Cubicle's-Eye View of Bosses, Meetings, Management Fads & Other Workplace Afflictions
author: Scott Adams
description: The book version of every meeting I've ever sat through. Queued for the next time work gets absurd.

title: The Judge's List
author: John Grisham
description: A judge with a kill list and an investigator who won't let go. A classic Grisham setup.

title: The Naked Sun
author: Isaac Asimov
description: The second Baley and Daneel mystery — a murder on a world where people can't stand to share a room. Another childhood read I want to revisit.

title: The New Yorker Book of Technology Cartoons (with CD-Rom)
author: Robert Mankoff
description: Cartoonists making fun of technology, which deserves it. For the coffee table more than the reading chair.

title: The Reckoning
author: John Grisham
description: Grisham goes historical — a decorated veteran shoots the town pastor and won't say why. The hook got me; the book is waiting.

title: The Robots of Dawn
author: Isaac Asimov
description: The third robot mystery, and the one that ties toward Foundation. Re-reading these in order alongside the rest of Asimov.

title: The Scorpio Illusion
author: Robert Ludlum
description: A Ludlum thriller from his prime years. Sometimes you just want the world saved at a breathless pace.

title: The Sentinel
author: Arthur C. Clarke
description: The short story that grew into 2001, plus other early Clarke. Worth having for the title story alone.

title: TIMES COMPLETE HISTORY OF THE WORLD
author: Richard Overy
description: The whole of history in one heavy volume. Not a book you finish — a book you keep visiting.

title: Unnatural Selections (The Far Side Series) (Spanish Edition)
author: Gary Larson
description: A Far Side collection I haven't got to yet. They've all earned the benefit of the doubt.

title: We Have No Idea: A Guide to the Unknown Universe
author: Jorge Cham,Daniel Whiteson
description: A cheerful tour of everything physics can't explain yet. Honest science with jokes — my kind of pairing.

title: Dragons of Eden
author: Carl Sagan
description: Sagan traces how the human brain came to be. Clear, curious, and humble — the way science writing should read.

title: “Surely You're Joking, Mr. Feynman!”: Adventures of a Curious Character
author: Richard Feynman
description: Feynman's stories about Feynman. I've heard these anecdotes second-hand for years; time for the source.

title: Accelerate
author: Nicole Forsgren, Jez Humble, Gene Kim
description:
Describes the results of several years of research into high functioning teams.
                    The authors, present several metrics that correlate with high functioning teams
                    and the factors that contribute to these metrics. It is an excellent case for
                    devops and automation.

title: Artemis
author: Andy Weir
description:
A thrilling story set on the moon, where a young woman named Jazz Bashara gets
                    involved in a heist that could change the future of the lunar city, Artemis.

title: At the Edge of Time: Exploring the Mysteries of Our Universe’s First Seconds
author: Dan Hooper
description:
The first few trillionths of a second after the big bang was an incredibly
                    active and mysterious time in our universe. Dan Hooper explores these few
                    moments and explain how our universe may have come to be. He takes us to the
                    edge of our current understanding and surveys the various unanswered questions
                    that scientists are struggling with today.

title: Collapse
author: Jared Diamond
description: Diamond on why societies fail, from Easter Island to Greenland. Sobering, but the case studies read like detective stories.

title: Creation: Life and How to Make It
author: Steve Grand
description:
The presentation is excellent. Builds up the universe from the most fundamental
                    particle up to life and intelligence. Reframes our understanding of the physical
                    universe as well as what it means to be intelligent. I wish there was more
                    technical discussion on the implementation of the brain. However, the high level
                    ideas are quite useful and goes a long way towards helping you understand the
                    requirements for building truly intelligent life.

title: Death's End
author: Cixin Liu
description:
A satisfying end to the saga. Wraps up the story well and introduces some hope
                    which was a relief after the dark outlook of the previous book. Surprisingly,
                    was able to continue introducing new concepts and exploring intriguing ideas up
                    to the end.

title: Holly
author: Stephen King
description:
A well written mystery with good old fashioned human monsters. The detective
                    work unfolded smoothly. Some of the side quests felt forced but overall, did not
                    detract from the story.

title: How to Expect the Unexpected: The Science of Making Predictions―and the Art of Knowing When Not To
author: Kit Yates
description: This book was featured on the Physics World podcast.

title: Milton Berle's Private Joke File: Over 10,000 of His Best Gags, Anecdotes, and One-Liners
author: Milton berle
description: Ten thousand jokes, most of them older than I am. You don't read it so much as raid it.

title: Now: The Physics of Time
author: Richard A. Muller
description:
This book explores the concept of time from a physics perspective. Richard A. Muller
                    delves into the nature of time, its relationship with space, and how it is perceived
                    by humans. The book provides a comprehensive understanding of time, combining
                    scientific theories with philosophical insights.

title: Project Hail Mary
author: Aandy Weir
description:
A well told science fiction story reminiscent of Heinlein. The main character is
                    a human who wakes up in a spaceship with no memory of how he got there. He must
                    figure out how to survive and find out what is going on.

title: Sleeping Beauties
author: Stephen King, Owen King
description: Easy flowing story telling. A gret premise and well executed.

title: The Age of Em: Work, Love and Life When Robots Rule the Earth
author: Robin Hanson
description: This is proving harder to read than I expected. I'm going to put it aside for now.

title: The Beginning of Infinity: Explanations That Transform the World
author: David Deutsch
description: This is proving harder to read than I expected. I'm going to put it aside for now.

title: The Blade Itself
author: Joe Abercrombie
description: Abercrombie's characters are all broken in interesting ways, which is not my usual taste — but the writing is so direct and alive that I didn't mind. Logen Ninefingers won me over.

title: A Little Hatred
author: Joe Abercrombie
description: The next Abercrombie generation, with the industrial revolution arriving. Harsher than my usual fare, but he writes people you can't look away from.

title: The Cartoon Guide to Statistics
author: Larry Gonick, Woollcott Smith
description: Statistics taught with cartoons. If it makes one confusing idea stick, it has done its job.

title: The Case Against Reality
author: Donald Hoffman
description: This is a well-argued stance on reality and our perception of reality. The basic concept is that we do not percieve the truth of reality, rather, we percieve reality in the way that maximises our chance of survival. The argument is that we create a reality when we observe the world. That reality that we create may not be the objective reality.

title: The Dark Forest
author: Cixin Liu
description: A bit dark. Proposes a bleak outlook on the relationship between civilizations in the universe. Incredibly well written.

title: The Far Side Gallery
author: Gary Larson
description:
I deliberately paced myself with this one. I limited myself to 2 pages per
                    night.

title: The Far Side Gallery 2
author: Gary Larson
description: The second big collection. Queued up for the same two-pages-a-night treatment as the first.

title: The Far Side Gallery 3
author: Gary Larson
description: The third collection. There's comfort in knowing more Larson is waiting.

title: The Math of Life and Death: 7 Mathematical Principles That Shape Our Lives
author: Kit Yates
description: Seven ways maths quietly runs everyday life. Yates was good on the podcast, so the book made the list.

title: The Quest for Eden
author: Elena Maria Marsella
description: An interesting exploration of the stories that we tell through the eyes of an Anthropologist. Re-interprets the myths of the past with new light. I'm probably not going to complete this book. It is brilliantly written and quite compelling but the last few chapters feel like a chore.

title: The Staff Engineer's Path
author: Tanya Reilly
description: Reilly maps the senior IC track clearly and without pretension. I recommend it to every engineer wondering what comes after 'senior'.

title: The Three-Body Problem
author: Cixin Liu
description: Profound is an understatment. This well told story introduced many unique concepts. Intelligent use of scientific knowledge.

title: The Visual Display of Quantitative Information, 2nd Ed.
author: Edward R. Tufte
description: The classic on showing data honestly. I've seen it quoted so often that reading the source felt overdue.

title: We Have No Idea: A Guide to the Unknown Universe
author: Jorge Cham, Daniel Whiteson
description: Cham and Whiteson on the universe's open questions. I like books that are upfront about what we don't know.

title: You Like It darker: Stories
author: Stephen King
description: King's newest story collection. His short fiction has been strong lately, so expectations are up.

title: The Devils
author: Joe Abercrombie
description: I’ve been enjoying The Devils so far — it’s good fun, well written, and has some genuinely sharp humour. Joe Abercrombie’s voice is as strong as ever, and the story moves along at a nice pace. That said, it feels a bit lighter than I expected and lacks gravitas. I found it hard to really invest in the quest itself, as it didn’t feel especially important or urgent. Still, I like what I’ve read so far, and it’s an entertaining read even if it hasn’t fully grabbed me yet.

title: Never Flinch
author: Stephen King
description: I enjoyed reading Never Flinch, but it didn’t quite live up to my expectations. The premise was interesting and I like Holly Gibney as a character, but parts of the story felt a bit forced, especially as it went on. There were moments where the tension worked well, but others where it dragged or felt overcomplicated. That said, it was still an engaging read overall, and I’m glad I picked it up — just not one of my favourite Stephen King books.

title: The Silmarillion
author: J.R.R. Tolkien
description: The Silmarillion was one of my favourite books growing up because it felt like reading the history of Middle-earth rather than a typical fantasy story. I really enjoyed how deep the lore goes and how serious the tone is, especially the creation stories and the long conflict over the Silmarils. The tragedy around Fëanor and the Elves made the world feel heavy and real. It’s not the easiest book to get through, but if you like detailed world-building, it’s worth the effort.

title: Python: Advanced Guide to Artificial Intelligence
author: Giuseppe Bonaccorso, Rajalingappaa Shanmugamani
description: A working guide I'm going through slowly, notebook open. Useful for filling gaps rather than cover-to-cover reading.

title: The Will of the Many
author: James Islington
description: A Roman-inspired fantasy with a magic system built on ceding your strength to those above you. The premise alone sold me.

title: The Strength of the Few
author: James Islington
description: The sequel, queued so there's no waiting once I finish the first. I've been burned by gaps between series books before.

title: Red Rising
author: Pierce Brown
description: Darrow and the colour-coded society of Mars. It comes so highly recommended that I'm saving it for a proper holiday read.
